summary: The post argues that engineering managers aspiring to become directors should understand that the director role is fundamentally different from management—not just a senior version of it. Directors run the business, manage managers rather than ICs, and must excel at managing up, out, and across the organization. Getting promoted internally is preferable to being hired into a director role at a new company, and candidates should proactively signal their career goals and demonstrate org-level impact.
key_points: A director is not a super-senior manager—it's a fundamentally different role focused on running the business and knitting technology with business objectives
Internal promotion is generally better than being hired into a director role at a new company, because context and relationships matter enormously
Managing up and out (horizontally with peers) becomes essential at the director level, not optional
Directors must help managers lead using their own unique strengths rather than imposing their personal leadership style
Aspiring directors should demonstrate org-level impact by fixing systemic problems, not just excelling within their team
money_quotes: We want people in senior leadership who want to be there and are honing their skills in anticipation of an opportunity. Not 'oops, I accidentally a VP.'
Blaming something on 'politics' is usually a way of accidentally confessing that you don't actually understand the constraints someone is operating under.

The distance between managing engineers and managing managers is nearly as vast as the gulf between being an engineer and being a manager. But it's sneakier, because you don't feel out of your depth as much as you did when you became a manager.
If one person gives you a piece of hard feedback, assume 10x as many will never tell you. Be grateful. The more power you are perceived to have, the less feedback you will ever hear.
Directors run the business. They are accountable for results. You can't be bopping in and writing or reviewing code, or tossing off technical opinions. That's not your job anymore.
key_insight: The leap from engineering manager to director requires a fundamental identity shift—from leading a high-performing team to running the business—and most managers underestimate how different the job really is.
